items.title: What proposed CGT changes mean for small business as ASX hits 7 week low

Plus, William Buck Tax Services Partner Todd Want breaks down the federal government’s proposed tax reforms after online memes claiming the Prime Minister would “own” 47 per cent of small businesses sparked confusion and debate.

items.title: Advocate's call for Royal Commission into femicide receives mixed response

It comes amid ongoing strain on women's shelters and other frontline services, with calls for greater funding. The government has embraced the aim of ending gendered violence within a generation - but advocates say that won't be achieved without greater effort.

items.title: ASX slumps to seven week slow as global inflation fears escalate

In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with David Scutt from StoneX Group Inc. about the market sell-off, the impact of the Middle East crisis on energy prices, and why investors are now bracing for possible interest rate rises in the United States after inflation climbed to a three-year high. The episode also looks at sharp losses for Brambles and major miners including BHP.

items.title: Labor defends budget housing tax changes despite critical polls

The Coalition has flagged its own bold policies on tax and housing, while the Treasurer is working to sell a trio of changes affecting negative gearing, capital gains and trusts. Two new opinion polls suggest his federal budget has been poorly received by voters - but Jim Chalmers has declared the long-term gains from controversial housing tax changes are worth the short-term political pain.

items.title: We asked what you wanted to know about the budget. Here are the answers
items.summary: SBS News asked audiences what mattered most to them in the federal budget. These were the key questions.

items.title: Critics pay out on PM for 'cowardly' gambling reforms
items.summary: The government has finally filed its formal response to the gambling inquiry chaired by the late MP Peta Murphy. But their response has drawn sharp criticism from advocates, who say it was dropped the day of the federal Budget - and that it doesn't go far enough.

items.title: President Donald Trump set to arrive in Beijing SBS | News in Easy English 13 May 2026
items.summary: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

items.title: Investors, renters react cautiously to Australia changing negative gearing, capital gains tax
items.summary: Speculation was rife in the lead up to this year's budget about what it would contain for housing investors. Now we know what Treasurer Jim Chalmers has planned for the capital gains tax discount, and negative gearing regime, both of which have been blamed for favouring investors over first home buyers and inflating prices. What do the changes mean for renters and potential buyers in the housing market?

items.title: 'Devoid of all military logic': Zelenskyy fumes as Russia targets Ukraine civilians
items.summary: The attacks came following the end of a three-day ceasefire to mark the Soviet victory in World War Two.

items.title: Why the budget might not 'shift the dial' on home ownership
items.summary: The government says its housing tax reforms will help first home buyers — but some are doubtful.

items.title: A drawing my son did at kindergarten made me quit my corporate job
items.summary: Terry Murton says his obsession with climbing the corporate ladder stopped him from being the father and husband he wanted to be.

items.title: Treasurer flags 'fairer' budget as business groups criticise tax reforms
items.summary: The Federal Budget has been handed down, including major tax changes that have been welcomed by unions, social services groups, and renters groups. Industry groups have raised concerns, saying the tax changes will be bad for business, as debate continues over how to balance fairness and fiscal restraint.

items.title: Treasurer cites 'hard road of reform' as he reveals 2026 federal budget
items.summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has delivered his 2026 budget, which he says takes what he's called the 'hard road of reform'. At the heart of the budget are housing tax changes, and a $35 billion saving from reform to the runaway National Disability Insurance Scheme. Labor has been keen to portray this budget as one of spending restraint, after warnings by economists and Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock that splashing out would only fuel inflation and risk further rate hikes.

items.title: 2026-27 federal budget migration numbers: What's changing and who's affected
items.summary: The budget has confirmed the number of permanent migrants to be allowed into Australia.

items.title: Your five-minute guide to the budget: Key changes Australians should know
items.summary: The key measures, major announcements and biggest takeaways from the federal budget — explained simply.

items.title: Who are the winners and losers of the 2026 federal budget?
items.summary: Small business owners, first home buyers, migrants and travellers are just some of the Australians impacted by the 2026-27 federal budget.

items.title: Where your tax money goes: Breaking down the federal budget as $100
items.summary: It's a huge budget — we've broken it down into a simple $100 snapshot to show where every dollar goes.

items.title: BHP reclaims crown as Australia's largest listed company
items.summary: BHP surged to a record high and overtook Commonwealth Bank as Australia’s largest listed company, helping lift the materials sector evenas the ASX200 fell for a third straight session. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Jonathan Shead from State Street Investment Management unpack BHP’s milestone, the strength in mining stocks and the broader investment environment. NAB Head of Australian Economics Gareth Spence also joins, to explain why business conditions have dropped to their weakest level since 2020 and how the conflict in the Middle East is driving up costs across supply chains.

items.title: Town 'at the end of the world' denies hantavirus origins as travellers warned
items.summary: Authorities have pointed the finger at an Argentine tourism hotspot, but locals have accused the government of a "smear campaign".

items.title: The 48-hour deadline DFAT faces for Australians returning from hantavirus-hit cruise
items.summary: Four Australians, a New Zealand citizen and one permanent resident will spend three weeks in a quarantine facility near Perth.

items.title: Scrolling through his phone after his marriage ended, Tim realised he had no one to call
items.summary: For some men, a lack of emotional resources means profound struggle and suicidal ideation can follow breakups.

items.title: What time is the 2026 federal budget? Here's everything we know so far
items.summary: Here are some of the big-ticket items that will impact Australians.

items.title: Why CSL sank, and what the Federal Budget could mean for investors
items.summary: The ASX 200 slipped 0.5 per cent ahead of the Federal Budget as oil prices surged and investors weighed the prospect of tax reform. Banks were weaker across the board, while CSL plunged 16 per cent after cutting earnings expectations and flagging a major impairment.

items.summary: Australians evacuated from the MV Hondius will quarantine for weeks in a pandemic-era facility outside Perth.

items.title: Australia's dying middle class — and the rise of an inherited wealth nation
items.summary: The wealth of Australia's middle-class has flatlined over five years but there's one group whose assets have soared.

items.title: One in two Australians is experiencing workplace burnout. Nicole found a solution
items.summary: What are the factors keeping many people in the rat race — and what are the ones driving others out?

items.title: 'Not another COVID': WHO urges calm ahead of evacuation of hantavirus-hit cruise ship
items.summary: Authorities in the Canary Islands are preparing to evacuate a cruise ship after a hantavirus outbreak killed three passengers.

items.title: 'Unfair, unacceptable': Jim Chalmers flags major tax changes in upcoming budget
items.summary: The treasurer also said Australians are paying a "hefty price" for the war in the Middle East, but pushed back on recession fears.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Health Minister Mark Butler ahead of the Budget
items.summary: There are big changes ahead for the NDIS with some budget cuts and tightened eligibility. Health Minister Mark Butler says the government aims to remove around 160,000 participants by the end of the decade. Children with autism are the largest cohort of NDIS participants, with many set to be moved into the government's new thriving kids program and he says future eligibility will be based on a different set of criteria. He says work is also being done on cultural safety before Indigenous participants are transitioned off the NDIS under the new reform measures. Mr Butler's been talking the SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson.

items.title: Maria has 13 children. It has its challenges, but there's one thing she's sure of
items.summary: Maria El Khoury's children describe her as a strong role model who is patient, selfless and caring.

items.title: Eleven million people, just 500 doctors. How Australia's neighbour manages healthcare
items.summary: It's Australia's closest neighbour and biggest aid recipient. But PNG's healthcare crisis has major impacts on patients and staff.

items.title: One Nation claims historic win in Farrer in poll seen as litmus test for Coalition
items.summary: Voters in the southern New South Wales seat of Farrer have headed to the polls to choose a new MP after their former representative Sussan Ley resigned from parliament. Analysts say the vote could have lasting consequences for the coalition.

items.title: How do Australians really feel about each other? New data paints an 'alarming' picture
items.summary: Widespread declines were observed in a survey exploring how much Australians trust each other and the country's institutions.

items.title: Iran accuses US of 'reckless military adventure' after empty oil tankers struck
items.summary: The US military claims it struck the ships in the Strait of Hormuz after they tried to breach its blockade on Iranian ports.

items.title: A $100 billion Australian sector is struggling. For Angela, it means 'working twice as hard'
items.summary: As costs soar for small businesses, tax relief and reduced red tape are tipped as support delivered in the federal budget.

items.title: Russia and Ukraine agree to renewed three-day ceasefire and prisoner swap, Trump says
items.summary: The US president said the halt in fighting for Victory Day celebrations could mark the "beginning of the end" of the war.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Jessica Rosenberg on encouraging young people to engage on quantum technologies
items.summary: How should Australia be preparing students for new technologies? It’s an issue governments and educators are grappling with across the world. American expert in the field, Professor Jessica Rosenberg from George Mason University, has been developing a school curriculum to encourage more young people to engage on quantum technologies. She’s also keen to see more girls and women in the field. During her visit to Australia she sat down with Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson to discuss the quantum task ahead.

items.title: A $100 billion Australian sector is struggling. For Angela, it means ‘working twice as hard’
items.summary: Soaring costs are hurting Australia’s 2.7 million small businesses – especially in hospitality. So, what can the federal government’s May budget offer?

items.title: Five key takeaways from the first week of antisemitism royal commission hearings
items.summary: As the antisemitism royal commission wraps up its first week of hearings, here is what the inquiry has heard so far.

items.title: 'Race to the bottom': Donald Trump, the midterm elections, and a battle over maps
items.summary: Battle lines have been drawn in anticipation of the United States midterm elections — quite literally.

items.title: 'Second Chance': Focus on children as IS group-linked families return to Australia
items.summary: A group of Australian women and children with links to the self-proclaimed IS group have returned to Australia after spending years in a Syrian detention camp. Three of the women have been arrested upon their arrival. Now, authorities say they are focused on the ensuring the wellbeing and reintegration of children among the group.

items.title: New crimes against humanity charges and $2 million question over IS-group-linked women
items.summary: The AFP is yet to answer questions about the fate of the children, many of whom were born in Middle Eastern prison camps.

items.title: An uneasy relationship with diversity emerges in Farrer ahead of One Nation vote test
items.summary: Eighteen months after white supremacists marched down Farrer's streets, the electorate's views on migration are being tested.

items.title: ASX soars, Aussie dollar at 4-year high on hopes of war’s end
items.summary: The Australian sharemarket rose 0.96 per cent, amid hopes of a peace deal between the US and Iran. As a result, oil prices have dropped sharply – though remain above US$100 a barrel – and precious metal and commodity prices have risen. The Australian dollar has also benefited, reaching a four-year high, as risk appetite increases. For more on this, Stephanie Youssef spoke with Angus Geddes, Chief Investment Officer at Fat Prophets.

items.title: Snow in May? What Australia's cold snap means for this winter
items.summary: Climate experts have shared predictions for this winter, as snow falls in parts of Australia.

items.title: Three IS-linked women arrested at Australian airports, expected to be charged
items.summary: A group of women and children linked to the so-called Islamic State have arrived in Melbourne and Sydney.

items.title: Is Trump’s ‘Epic Fury’ over? IS group 'brides' return home & a flatpack forehead slap?!
items.summary: The latest pause in the US-Iran conflict has the world holding its breath - has the White House lost control of its own strategy? National security concerns surround the return of IS-linked women and children to Australia and a major election victory for Narendra Modi sparks claims of election fraud in India. Plus, the surprisingly brutal world of competitive furniture building.

items.title: Healing through art : Stories of First Nations people who have experienced incarceration
items.summary: First Nations Australians who have lived experience of incarceration in Victoria are showcasing their work through an art exhibition. The exhibition, highlighting the artists' voices, has proved the ripple effect - where one event can cause a series of bigger effects - for some to take a new path beyond prison.

items.title: 'Will not please everyone': Government details reservation scheme for gas exporters
items.summary: While some stakeholders have described it as "a winner", others have criticised the proposed policy as a distraction.

items.title: 'Incredible': IS-linked family speaks from plane as cohort set to soon arrive in Australia
items.summary: The families are set to arrive in Melbourne and Sydney today and police have said there will likely be some arrests when they land.

items.title: WHO says hantavirus is not the new COVID, as passengers evacuated from outbreak cruise ship
items.summary: The cruise ship linked to a deadly hantavirus outbreak has left Cape Verde and is headed for the Canary Islands, where passengers are expected to depart the vessel and begin heading home. Three people have died in the outbreak with eight recorded cases so far. But the World Health Organisation is moving to reassure people hantavirus is not the new COVID-19.

items.title: Russia-Ukraine ceasefire crumbles within hours after drone, missile attacks reported
items.summary: Ukraine reported Russian drone attacks just hours after a unilaterally announced ceasefire was supposed to begin.

items.title: 'You're building something tangible': The offline trend pulling people off screens
items.summary: A new generation is picking up the threads, turning knitting into a statement on sustainability and self-expression.

items.title: Fiji, Australia ink treaty over climate change
Rising living costs hitting some older Australians hardest and the Australian dollar at fresh 4 year high
items.summary: Senior Australians on government payments are facing some of the sharpest cost of living increases in the country as soaring electricity prices and rising interest rates put pressure on household budgets. In this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Patricia Sparrow from COTA Australia to find out more, before Laura Cooper from Nuveen shares her outlook on interest rates after the Reserve Bank’s latest decision. Plus, Luke Laretive from Seneca Financial Solutions breaks down a strong rally on the Australian sharemarket as the ASX200 surged following another record session on Wall Street.

items.title: 'A different world': What the Reserve Bank's three future scenarios mean for Australia
items.summary: There are three possible outlooks for the Australian economy that the RBA is considering — one "difficult" and two worse.

items.title: Eta Aquarids will light up Australian skies. Here's how to see the meteor shower
items.summary: The meteor shower will decorate Australian skies, but experts warn light pollution could hinder city views.

items.title: Roblox recruitment: Why so many kids are falling into extremism
items.summary: Online gaming platforms like Roblox and Minecraft have become increasingly popular tools for extremist groups to recruit young people. With Australia's terror threat level at 'probable', the government has announced a new Counter Terrorism Online Centre to disrupt and divert terrorist networks from radicalising young Australians.

items.title: Bondi accused charged with 19 new offences while in custody
items.summary: The new charges include ten counts of shooting with intent to murder during Australia's deadliest mass shooting in almost three decades.

items.title: A group of women and children linked to IS Group returning to Australia | SBS News in Easy English 6 May 2026
AFL to introduce new mental health rules after record fine to Carlton
items.summary: Carlton have been fined $75,000 over their handling of an incident involving player Elijah Hollands in a game against Collingwood in April. The fine will be paid to the AFL's mental health partner, Headspace. The finding has highlighted the treatment of mental health within sporting circles.

items.title: Teen radicalisation fears drive major online counter‑terror overhaul
items.summary: Spies will monitor gaming platforms as authorities warn extremists are targeting young people through gaming platforms and chat groups.

items.title: Trump pauses day-old Hormuz operation in latest push for Iran deal
items.summary: The so-called "Project Freedom" was supposed to help vessels depart the Strait of Hormuz under US guard.

items.title: Sina was forced to weigh a 'dreaded' decision — and fears the rate hike will spark another
items.summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia has warned that the economic outlook is not good for Australians.

items.title: Interest rates rise again as markets slip and mortgage pressure builds
items.summary: The Reserve Bank has lifted interest rates for the third time this year, pushing the cash rate to 4.35 per cent, even as other major economies pause. In this episode, PWC Chief Economist Amy Lomas unpacks what’s driving the decision and what it signals for the months ahead. The ASX edged lower, falling 0.2 per cent, with Todd Hoare from LGT Wealth Management breaking down how investors reacted. And with borrowers facing rising costs, Sally Tindall from Canstar explains what the latest rate hike means for mortgage repayments and how to assess whether your loan is still competitive.

items.title: Antisemitism hearing told man's public assault largely ignored on busy street
items.summary: Nir Golan now covers his kippah with a baseball cap.

items.title: Seven cases of hantavirus now identified on cruise ship stranded off west Africa
items.summary: Seven cases of the deadly hantavirus have now been identified in a suspected outbreak on a luxury cruise ship off West Africa. The outbreak has caused global concern, as the disease is considered rare among humans. What is happening with the ship, and what does hantavirus actually entail?

items.title: Does the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion go far enough?
items.summary: The first two days of public hearings for the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has heard from 24 witnesses who are Jewish Australians with lived experience of antisemitism. While there is broad community support for a Royal Commission, some argue it should be expanded to include other minority perspectives including Muslim and First Nations Australians' experiences of racism.
